This one's exciting. This is about creating and utilizing your own personal question and answer database. So in all circumstances, in all circumstances, questions about your program, about the same things over and over, two separate things, are probably worth including into your onboarding process, okay? Less common questions that you get should still 100% be answered and stored away. They will get used later. That being said, please live by this rule. The question and answer database is not for the clients. The question and answer database is for you. There should be a healthy and growing database of less common questions, okay? Basically any question you answer, you store it away, and you should frequently pull from it, but you shouldn't necessarily be pulling from the same questions all the time from the database, okay? It works best when it's done on demand and it's done on the client's behalf, okay? I'll get into what that means in a second. So the Q&A database ideally should be in both written and video format. So the first method is that you can easily respond to a client's email or voxter or whatever with a video and then store that video away. So if somebody answers a question, asks you a question, you answer it in the form of a video, and then you take that video and you put it in a repository. And then you can create a written explanation to that question from that video soon after that. So you have both a written and a video format of that question. And the second method is the equal opposite. It's that you can have a written response to a question that you get and then store that in a way in a big Mondo document, and then you can create a video explanation of the response soon after that. So either way, you get both methods of communication. Yeah, everybody gets the same questions, but it's important that when you get a question, you record it in your own words, in your own voice, in your own flavor. Some people are spicier than others. And so answer the questions as you get them stored away, and it'll be in your voice and in your format. And that's a positively great way to build rapport with clients. Yes, I understand that most all the questions that you get are Google-able and it's frustrating because again, people can just look it up on their own, but they don't. But you have to remember, there is a reason why the client is coming to you. This is good. Would you rather they didn't come to you? You're the expert. You're the expert. Have flavorful answers to all the basic questions and make clients happy. Happy clients are motivated clients. Motivated clients get things done. When clients get things done, they get results. This is all good. So there are two main reasons why having written and video forms of the answers is important, mostly because some people prefer to read and some people prefer to watch or listen. Secondarily, the document that you keep all of your answers in becomes searchable. So links to the videos are placed within the written answers. And so you can control F, wham, bam, you got your answer right there. Should the question and answer database be available to the client? We kind of discuss this already, but probably not. No, in principle, it shouldn't hurt. But I have seen that when it's not presented and executed properly, it provides a false sense of security for the practitioner. I see clients get neglected because information is theirs for the taking and they don't take it. You ever made an enormous video repository like a membership portal and give people the link and then nobody ever logs in. And then they're asking you questions about stuff that's in the portal. This is exactly my point. Okay. Head this off with the past and use it as an opportunity to build a relationship rather than being frustrating at the person's supposed incompetence. Okay. It's kind of like, well, I can train at home, but because you can train at home, you can train it at any time and then you never end up doing it. Same difference. They may neglect to ask questions because there's a database available, but they simultaneously neglect to reference it. So in either case, that story ends in confusion and heartache and drop off and frustration and all these other words. So cobbling together some crappy PDF that consists of copied and pasted emails and calling it a new book is a great way to insult your clients and shoot yourself in the foot. Same way of having some ridiculous 20 minute long Q and A video that you expect. Slap clients to sit through and watch. Nobody ever will. It makes both people look goofy. Okay. It's like, well, I sent them the Q and A stuff. My job is done. I'm just going to sit back and collect a monthly payment. Like that's, that's basically what you're communicating to people. They won't read it or watch it because it sucks and they won't follow the directions you provided because they didn't watch it and the client will appear incompetent despite your efforts at educating them. All right. Both parties become irritated and resentful. They have everything they need. They'll be fine. Right. It's a great excuse to be lazy and then blend the client if they fail and that's ridiculous. Not to mention a frustrated client that falls off the wagon is a loss of income. So let's be real there. So think of it this way. Think of it this way. If you eliminate a conversation, then you eliminate your ability to build a relationship. Having a pre-prepared answer to a question is perfectly fine. This is how you systemize the Q and A process. When you're the one responding to the question, you have the ability to mold the pre-made answer to the client and humanize the interaction. When it comes to client interactions, automate nothing, systemize everything.
